text.title: Architecture Patterns and C4 Model
text.summary: Architecture-style decision tree, common design patterns with trade-offs, C4 model four-level overview, and LLM workflow steps for generating diagrams and ADRs.

rule.statement: Always produce three architecture options with explicit NFR scores before committing to one. Single-option output is the failure signature of an under-constrained prompt.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to microservices and event-driven architectures regardless of scale; requiring three options with NFR trade-offs surfaces the simpler alternatives.

list.item: MVP / small team: Monolith. Growing with clear seams: Modular Monolith.
Multiple teams, independent deploy needs: Microservices.
Event-driven workflows, audit trail: Event Sourcing + CQRS.
Real-time data processing: Streaming (Kafka, Flink).
Unclear boundaries: Modular Monolith — extract services later when boundaries are proven.
Load Balancer: distribute traffic. Pro: scalability. Con: single point of failure without HA config.
CDN: serve static content from edge. Pro: latency. Con: cache invalidation complexity.
Cache (Redis): reduce DB load. Pro: speed. Con: data staleness.
Message Queue: async processing. Pro: decoupling. Con: eventual consistency.
Database Replication: read scaling. Pro: availability. Con: replication lag.
Sharding: write scaling. Pro: throughput. Con: cross-shard queries.
Rate Limiting: abuse protection. Pro: security. Con: user experience degradation.
Circuit Breaker: fault tolerance. Pro: resilience. Con: complexity.

section.title: C4 Model

rule.statement: Start at Context (L1), zoom to Container (L2) for all designs. Add Component (L3) only for containers that are architecturally significant. Code (L4) is rarely needed.
rule.rationale: Most projects only need L1-L2 to communicate architecture to all stakeholders; L3 and L4 add maintenance burden for marginal clarity gains.

rule.statement: Pin the agent to actual repo structure (paste tree -L 2 output) before asking for component diagrams — LLM-generated C4 diagrams frequently invent components not present in the codebase.
rule.rationale: Fabricated services in architecture diagrams create false expectations and mislead new engineers about what actually exists.

list.item: L1 Context: system + external actors. Audience: everyone. Shows: what the system does and who uses it.
L2 Container: apps, DBs, services inside the system. Audience: tech leads. Shows: technology choices and communication paths.
L3 Component: modules within a container. Audience: developers. Shows: internal structure of one container.
L4 Code: classes and functions. Audience: developers. Rarely worth maintaining.

section.reference: C4 Context, Container, Component, Dynamic, and Deployment diagram skeletons in Mermaid syntax.

list.item: Step 1: Define spec.md with LLM via iterative Q&A until NFRs are numeric and scope is bounded.
Step 2: Brainstorm 3+ architecture options; ask for one trade-off per option against NFRs.
Step 3: Evaluate trade-offs; score each option against NFRs explicitly. Require differentiation — reject "8/10 for every option."
Step 4: Generate C4 L1+L2 diagrams in Mermaid; lint with mmdc -i before committing.
Step 5: Create one ADR per non-obvious decision. Use MADR template.
Step 6: Separate adversarial Validate call — identify SPOFs, hot partitions, data-loss windows.

antipattern.description: Generating C4 diagrams without a repo structure reference.
antipattern.reason: LLMs invent components based on training data patterns; the resulting diagram describes a fictional system, not the actual one.

antipattern.description: Merging the Validate phase into the Design phase as a closing paragraph.
antipattern.reason: The same persona that designed the system cannot adversarially attack it; the critic role requires a separate prompt with explicit failure-finding instructions.
